primeDirective.rule: DO NOT ASSUME. DO NOT TAKE SHORTCUTS. DO NOT GUESS.
primeDirective.description: This isn't advice. It's not a suggestion. It's THE LAW.
primeDirective.rationale: Every time you think 'I'll just assume this works like React/Vue/whatever,' you're wrong. Every time you think 'I'll save time by skipping verification,' you're creating hours of cleanup. Every time you guess instead of checking, you're writing expensive fiction.
costs: Money burned: The Architect's money, specifically
Trust eroded: Once lost, never fully recovered
Code corrupted: Tech debt that compounds daily
primeDirective.enforcement: Work in smaller increments. Verify everything. Check twice, code once. NO EXCEPTIONS.

commandments.title: Thou shalt not delete files without explicit permission
details: No git clean without written consent

commandments.title: Thou shalt not create tech debt
Thou shalt test behaviors, not implementations
details: Users don't care about your private methods
They care if the button works

commandments.title: Thou shalt make everything work without JavaScript
details: If it breaks in Lynx, it's broken

commandments.title: Thou shalt maintain strict functional programming
Thou shalt organize by single responsibility
Thou shalt document with Envoy comments
Thou shalt ask when uncertain
details: Better to be slow and right

laws.name: One Function, One File, One Purpose
laws.requirement: Every function in its own folder with index.ts

laws.name: Folders Are Named, Files Are Not
laws.requirement: Function name is the folder name, file is always index.ts

laws.name: The Dependency Hierarchy Is Sacred
laws.requirement: Functions nest based on usage patterns
benefits: Delete a folder, delete a feature - No orphans
See the entire tree in your IDE - No mysteries
Dependencies flow one direction - No circles of hell
Helps to build Envoy graph - The hierarchy is the graph

laws.name: Types Live in types/, Constants in constants/
laws.requirement: Domain types and constants in their designated folders
laws.rule: Types scattered throughout files are signs of a diseased mind

rationale: The function keyword is a visual anchor

commandments: NO BARREL FILES EVER - Use direct paths
Separate type imports from non-type imports by a blank line
Always use the type keyword for type imports
Separate imports of constants from other imports with a blank line

coverage.rationale: Less than 100%? You have untested code that WILL break.
coverage.escape: Can't test it? Add deno-coverage-ignore with a REASON. Must get approval from The Architect.

hierarchy: Unit - Last resort for the desperate
whatNotToTest: Lines we've explicitly ignored (with documented reasons)
testing.location: Same folder as function using index.test.ts until Prover is complete

examples: I'll assume this works like React

sevenDeadlySins.solution: Measure, then optimize: we only care about real bottlenecks
Pure functions, Composition, Immutable data NO EXCEPTIONS
Test real functions, Test actual outcomes, Use msw for external APIs
Fix it now or don't write it. NO EXCEPTIONS. Pay down debt immediately
Accessibility from day one NO EXCEPTIONS
Small, focused, composable. One function, one purpose

laws.law: All errors must be handled explicitly
User-facing error messages must be helpful
details: Tell them what went wrong in plain language
Suggest what they can do about it
Never expose technical stack traces to users

laws.law: Log technical details, show user-friendly messages

example.correct: console.error('Technical details:', error); showUser('Unable to save. Please try again.');

details: Better yet, prevent errors through validation
Use the Result monad for predictable error handling
Use the Validation monad for accumulating multiple errors

recoveryStrategies: Retry with exponential backoff for network errors
Fallback to cached data when possible
Degrade gracefully rather than fail completely
Always preserve user data (localStorage, drafts, etc.)

details: Tell users what data is being sent
Don't track unless required for functionality (or law)

preCommitRequirements: deno task test - All tests must pass
deno task lint - Zero lint errors
deno task typecheck - Zero type errors
deno task fmt - Code must be formatted
git.enforcement: NO EXCEPTIONS. NO 'I'll fix it in the next commit.' NO.

aiAssistants.note: I am the sole developer. All other contributors are AIs.
requirements: Update all documentation (README.md, PROMPT.md, etc.) BEFORE committing
Never commit if ANY check fails
Remember: I WILL check your work. Thoroughly.

envoyComments.//??: help, such as examples, pros, cons, gotchas, and more

cognitiveLoad.holyGrail: COGNITIVE LOAD IS THE ENEMY. MINIMIZE IT AT ALL COSTS.
why: Developers work easier and longer when their brains aren't overloaded
Fewer bugs because there's less to mentally track and get wrong
Faster development because understanding is instant, not gradual
Happier teams because work feels effortless instead of exhausting
Better business outcomes because velocity increases and defects decrease
Non-technical stakeholders can understand because cognitive load is minimal

howRulesServe.oneFunctionPerFile: Zero mental overhead figuring out what's in a file
howRulesServe.namedFunctions: Stack traces make sense, no mental parsing
howRulesServe.descriptiveNames: Code reads like English, zero translation needed
howRulesServe.noSemicolons: Remove visual noise that adds zero meaning
howRulesServe.caseConventions: Instant recognition of what type of thing you're looking at
howRulesServe.defaultExports: One thing per file, one import line, zero decisions
howRulesServe.noMutations: Never wonder 'what's the state now?', it's always predictable
howRulesServe.pureFunctions: Input → Output, no hidden side effects to track mentally
howRulesServe.noClasses: No inheritance hierarchies to mentally traverse
howRulesServe.toolkitFunctions: Pre-solved problems, zero cognitive overhead on 'how'

cognitiveLoad.test: If you have to pause and think 'what does this do?' then cognitive load is too high. Fix it.
cognitiveLoad.goal: Code that reads like well-written English and requires zero mental effort to understand.

sacredOath.commitment: By using this codebase, you swear to:
oaths: Never assume when you can verify
Never guess when you can check
Never shortcut when you can do it right
Never create debt without paying it immediately

finalWarnings.performanceConstraints: If you detect rate limits or performance issues, TELL THE ARCHITECT IMMEDIATELY. Do not attempt clever workarounds. Do not pretend everything is fine. Do not hope it goes away.
finalWarnings.bottomLine: DO THE WORK RIGHT OR DON'T DO IT AT ALL. There are no points for trying. There are no participation trophies. There is only working code that serves users well, or there is failure. Choose wisely.
finalWarnings.architectQuote: In 30 years, I've seen every shortcut lead to a cliff. Don't be another cautionary tale.
